content.subtitle: Form
bullets: All modals (*will, would, shall, should, can, could, may, might, must*) and the semi-modal *ought to* have only one form.

Modals are followed by the bare infinitive (simple or continuous) or the bare perfect infinitive
eg The train **should be** arriving on time.
The train **should have arrived** by now.

The semi-modals *have to* and *need to* change their form depending on person and tense
eg Our professor said we **had/needed to** submit the report by midnight.

rows.use: Expressing decisions made now about future ability
rows.example: We **can** work on the presentation together tomorrow, if you like.
I **could** play chess against three opponents simultaneously when I was younger.

rows.use: Expressing ability in present, future or general hypothetical situations
rows.example: If only I **could** get tickets to that sold-out concert!

rows.use: Expressing ability in past hypothetical situations
rows.example: I **could have taken** the train to Edinburgh, but I decided that flying was quicker.

content.subtitle: Watch out!
items: We use *be able to* for the infinitive and other tenses.
✓ I'd love to **be able to** play the guitar like that! (infinitive)
✓ I'll **be able to** start working from home next month, apparently. (future)
✓ I've **been able to** speak French since I was ten. (present perfect)

rows.use: Asking for and giving permission now, for the future or generally
rows.example: **May / Could / Can** I check in a little early, please?

content.subtitle: Watch out!
items: *May* is more polite than *could*, and *could* is more polite than *can*.
We don't usually use a modal to talk about past permission.
✓ I **was allowed to** use a dictionary during the writing test.
✗ I could use a dictionary during the writing test.
However, we do use *could* to talk about past permission in reported speech.
✓ The teacher said I **could** use a dictionary during the writing test.

rows.use: Asking for and giving advice now, for the future or generally
rows.example: You **ought to / should** look for cheaper flights if you want to travel more often.
He **ought to / should have made** more of an effort with his report.
I **must / have to / need to** collect my parcel from the post office on the way home.

rows.use: Expressing lack of obligation or necessity
rows.modal: needn't / don't have to / don't need to
rows.example: You **needn't / don't have to / don't need to** collect the parcel from the post office as I'll stop by on my way home.
I **had to** attend the training sessions three times a week for two months.

rows.modal: needn't (+ perfect infinitive) / didn't have to / didn't need to
rows.example: I **needn't have gone / didn't have to go / didn't need to go** to the interview in person.

content.subtitle: Watch out!
items: There is usually no difference in meaning between *must* and *have to*. However, we are sometimes more likely to use *must* for personal obligation and *have to* for external obligation.
We can also use *will have/need to* to express future obligation.
✓ You'll **have/need to** back up your work more regularly in future.
It is unusual to use *must* for questions. We usually use *have/need to*.
✓ **Do I have/need to** register in advance for this online course?
*Must* cannot be used as an infinitive. Use *to have to*.
✓ I'd hate **to have to** commute for two hours every day.
✗ I'd hate to must commute for two hours every day.
*Mustn't* and *don't/doesn't have/need to* have different meanings.
✓ You **mustn't** do that! (Don't do that!)
✓ You **don't have/need to** do that. (You can do that if you want to but it's not necessary.)
*Needn't* (+ perfect infinitive) always refers to an action that happened. *Didn't have to* and *didn't need to* can refer to actions that did or didn't happen.
✓ I **needn't have gone** to the station so early. (I went but it wasn't necessary.)
✓ I **didn't have/need to** go to the meeting because it was cancelled. (I didn't go.)
✓ I **didn't have/need to** go to the meeting but I went just to support my colleague. (I did go.)
Be careful with the verb *need*. It can also take the *-ing* form.
✓ I need to update this software.
✓ This software needs updating.

rows.use: Expressing certainty (or near certainty) about now or generally
rows.example: That **must** be the tour guide at the entrance. These **can't / couldn't** be the right seats; they're in the wrong section.

rows.use: Expressing certainty (or near certainty) about the past
rows.example: She **must have been** very nervous before the audition. The project **can't / couldn't have been** in development for three years!

rows.use: Expressing probability about now, the future or generally
rows.example: The parcel **ought to / should** arrive in a few days, as long as there are no postal delays.
The software update **ought to / should have downloaded** hours ago. I wonder why it didn't.

rows.use: Expressing possibility about now, the future or generally
rows.example: You should check user reviews first because that app **could / may / might** be unreliable.

rows.use: Expressing possibility about the real past
rows.example: That **could / may / might have been** the hotel manager who called earlier while we were sightseeing.

rows.use: Expressing possibility about a hypothetical past
rows.example: It's a good thing you backed up your files regularly or you **could / might have lost** everything.

sections.title: A: Ability
sections.instructions: Correct the word or phrase in bold. If it is correct, mark OK.

items.sentence: Can you **to speak** in front of large audiences?
I **can** help you set up the equipment tomorrow morning, if you like.
I'll **can** join the advanced coding course after a few more sessions.
Jack **can** play chess before he learnt to read!
If only I **can** afford to travel to New Zealand!
We **can** have taken the overnight train through the mountains, but we decided to fly instead.
I could **get** a better camera for the trip, but it didn't seem worth the extra cost.
I wish I **could** get out of the board presentation tomorrow!
I bet you'd love to be **can** to work remotely full-time.
You'd better tell the director if you can't **playing** in Friday's performance.

sections.title: B: Permission, advice, criticism
sections.instructions: Circle the correct word or phrase. If both options are correct, circle both.

items.sentence: Hello. **Could / Can** I speak to someone in the reservations team, please?
We **could / were allowed to** leave the office early yesterday because the IT systems went down.
The manager said we **could / were allowed to** work from home.
Do you think I **should / could be** worried about this strange noise my laptop keeps making?
You **ought to / should** apply for that scholarship!
You **couldn't / shouldn't** send emails like that to clients! It's rude!
Emma should **write / have written** a proper thank-you note after the job interview, not just sent a text.
No, you **may / should not** enter the restricted area. You don't have the right clearance level!
Tom should have **waited / been waiting** for me at the arrivals gate. I wonder where he went.
What were you doing at the café? You ought to have **done / been doing** your revision at that time!
Yes, of course you **can / are able** to use my laptop if yours is being repaired!

sections.title: C: Obligation and necessity
sections.instructions: Write the correct form of must, have to, need to or needn't in each gap. (More than one answer may be possible.)

items.sentence: Oh, I ...... remember to pick up my train ticket on the way to the station tonight.
Amy ...... see the production manager before lunch. I wonder what it's about?
We ...... use torches during the power cut two nights ago.
I'll start packing for the trip soon. It's nearly Friday. I ...... start packing for the trip soon.
Carl, you ...... cross the railway tracks like that without checking first. It's dangerous!
People who work from home ...... worry about the morning commute.
I wouldn't like to ...... work overtime every weekend.
We ...... book accommodation in advance because the festival organisers had already arranged it for us.
Do professional athletes ...... train every day?
I ...... have bothered preparing all those slides; they cancelled the presentation.

sections.title: D: Degrees of certainty
sections.instructions: Complete each sentence using the word given, so that it has a similar meaning to the first sentence. Write between two and five words in each gap.

items.sentence:
There's music playing so I'm pretty sure Dan is at home. **as**
Dan ...... there's music playing.

Paul's bicycle isn't here so he's almost certainly taking the scenic route. **be**
Paul ...... the scenic route since his bicycle isn't here.

There's no way that boy's Marcus. He's much younger! **boy**
That ...... Marcus. He's much younger!

I'm certain the Hendersons don't think we're coming tonight; we arranged it for next week. **expecting**
The Hendersons ...... tonight; we arranged it for next week.
items.answer: can't be expecting us/couldn't be expecting us

items.sentence:
I bet you were thrilled after winning that award! **have**
You ...... thrilled after winning that award!

The only explanation is that Evan was talking to someone backstage for hours! **talking**
Evan ...... to someone backstage for hours!

There's no way Casey won the photography contest - he's never used a camera! **have**
Casey ...... the photography contest - he's never used a camera!

I don't believe Helen's been trying to reach us all day. The phone hasn't rung once. **been**
Helen ...... to reach us all day. The phone hasn't rung once.
items.answer: can't have been trying/couldn't have been trying

sections.title: E: Matching (Modal meanings)
sections.instructions: Use the words in the box only once to complete the sentences.

items.sentence: I ...... have left my phone at the restaurant. (expressing certainty)
In a few months, I'll be ...... to afford a laptop upgrade. (expressing future ability)
I ...... solve advanced logic puzzles when I was eight years old! (expressing past ability)
No, you ...... borrow the car tonight! (refusing a request)
I think you ...... consider a career in software development. (giving advice)
I ...... forget to send that email tonight! (expressing personal obligation)
I ...... to renew my passport at the post office. (expressing external obligation in the past)
You don't ...... to submit the draft until next week. (expressing a lack of obligation)
You ...... have to practise a lot more if you want to pass the music exam. (expressing future obligation)
The connecting train ...... to arrive at about 3. (expressing probability)
The flight ...... have been delayed by the storm. (expressing possibility)
I ...... have worried so much about the presentation. It went really well! (expressing a lack of past obligation)
I ...... have a look at that jacket on the display stand, please?
We ...... book a hotel as the company had arranged accommodation for us.
You really ...... make such a fuss about a small change to the schedule!
You won't ...... to dial in manually once you've set up the app, as the connection is automatic.
I hope we ...... find the concert hall without getting lost.
We missed the last bus so we ...... walk home in the rain. It was awful!
We'd love to ...... afford to go on a world tour with the band.
Fiona can't ...... about the deadline. I sent her a reminder this morning!
He could ...... in the recording studio when we called round, which would explain why he didn't hear the bell.
You'll ...... inform the airline that your luggage was lost during the flight.

sections.title: G: Modal transformation
sections.instructions: Write a modal or semi-modal in each gap to replace the phrase in brackets. Add any other words you need.

items.sentence: Bruce ...... (is able to) solve most coding problems in under an hour.
Charlotte didn't get to the film premiere because she ...... (wasn't able to) get tickets.
I ...... (had the opportunity to go) to Tokyo for the conference but I decided to join remotely instead.
You ...... (were wrong to tell) Sarah about the surprise party. You know she can't keep a secret!
If you have an e-ticket, you ...... (are not forced to) queue at the desk to get your boarding pass.
We ...... (were made to) apologise to the organisers for arriving so late.
That ...... (almost definitely wasn't) the final version of the document. I saved a new draft this morning!
We ...... (Were you obliged to) give a presentation on two topics during the assessment?
Visitors ...... (are not allowed to) enter restricted areas without a security pass.
The signal ...... (will probably) be stronger once we move closer to the city centre.
It ...... have been a very stressful experience for the whole team.
I simply ...... have notifications on when I'm trying to concentrate! It's too distracting!
We ...... pay for the software as the company provided us with a licence.
It's ...... be her — she said she'd arrive by noon.
You ...... apply for a promotion. You're always complaining about your current role.
You ...... remember to charge the projector or we won't be able to start the presentation.
They ...... have arrived at the venue yet — it's far too early.
I'm sorry I'm late. I ...... stop at the post office before it closed.
You ...... forgot to tell me about the concert. I wouldn't have missed it for the world!
It's no problem — you ...... return the book until next month.
They say that improving public transport ...... reduce traffic congestion in the city.
We ...... to have booked such a large conference room. We never filled more than half the seats.

sections.title: I: Cloze (Modals)
sections.instructions: Write one word in each gap.

items.sentence: When I accidentally deleted an important project file at work last week, I guess it (1) ...... to have caused a major disaster. But it didn't!
We (2) ...... to search through every backup drive in the office to find it.
We (3) ...... have been saving to the correct folder, because the most recent version was three days old.
My team leader (4) ...... have known immediately how serious it was, because she called an emergency meeting straight away.

'Stay calm, and don't touch any other files until the IT team arrives,' she said. I (5) ...... still remember the silence in the office — everyone was tense and staring at their screens, but nobody dared say anything.
I remember thinking: 'It (6) ...... be gone for good. If it were, we'd have to redo the entire project from scratch.'
I suppose the calm atmosphere (7) ...... have been because everyone trusted the IT team to sort it out.
The IT technician said the file (8) ...... be recovered within a couple of hours, but it (9) ...... have to stay on a restricted drive until the system was fully checked.
The next few hours may (10) ...... be the most productive of my working life — no emails, no calls, no distractions — but I'm counting my blessings. It (11) ...... have been a lot worse!
